title: Preserving the Khmer Smile: Classifying and Restoring the Faces of ...
highlights: Abstract The Bayon temple is known for its numerous massive stone faces with serene smiles, often referred to as the 'Khmer Smile.' Many of these sculptures are, however, only partially preserved, making it difficult to see the original appearance of these faces. To restore the Bayon faces, we propose a novel method that builds upon the matrix recovery theory. The method achieves accurate restoration by adopting a two-step shape recovery strategy. title: Classification of Bayon Faces
highlights: Digital 3D models of historic buildings or cultural heritage objects are useful for preservation. Not only can we store them permanently, but the models can supply a clear guideline for the restoration process. 3D models also provide sufficient information about geometrical characteristics that may help archaeologists to inspect and classify the objects. Currently, we are working on a 3D digital-archiving project of the Bayon Temple. It is a building of stonework that was built in the 12th century in Cambodia.
